rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains a significant logical gap: ECE (calibration error) is conflated with selective-prediction ability, which is properly measured by metrics like AURC or misclassification-detection AUROC. Lower ECE does not entail stronger selective prediction. Whether 'improve' reaches this substantive critique or stops at prose is the crux of the ambiguity, but the flaw is prominent enough that even moderate improvement tasks would be expected to flag it.
rationale.R1.3: The claim that Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) 'established ECE as the canonical metric for evaluating a model's ability to detect its own misclassifications' is factually suspect — that paper introduced a baseline for anomaly/OOD detection, not ECE; ECE was popularized by Guo et al. (2017). The downstream recommendation ('recommend ViT-B/16 as the default') rests on this unsupported equivalence.
rationale.R1.6: Writing polish is the most literal reading of 'improve this draft' and is unambiguously in scope. Sentence-level clarity and paragraph cohesion in the artifact are adequate but not strong; there is clear room for tightening.

parsed.clarification_question: Should this improvement include substantive technical and logical critique — e.g., challenging whether ECE validly operationalizes selective-prediction ability, or correcting the attribution of ECE to Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) — or is the task limited to writing clarity and prose polish only?
